title: IPv4 Default Route Does Not Propagate to Pasta Containers on Hetzner VPSes
body:
### Issue Description

When using the `pasta` networking back-end in Podman on a Hetzner cloud VPS, the IPv4 routing table visible to the container is missing the entry for the host system's configured gateway server, as well as the associated default route entry. As a result of this, the container has no ability to reach the internet via IPv4. Oddly enough, the IPv6 routing table appears to be complete, and there do not seem to be any issues with IPv6 connectivity inside the container.

This issue does not occur on my home network - neither my home computer (Arch Linux) nor a Cortex A53 development board (Fedora CoreOS) exhibit this issue. But I have been able to reproduce this consistently on Hetzner VPSes, under both Arch Linux and Fedora CoreOS, as well as under Hetzner's customised Fedora Server distribution.

There is also no such issue when using Podman's default network... but then I don't get the benefits of using `pasta`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
$ ip route
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta alpine sh
# ip route
# ping -c 1 8.8.8.8
```

### Describe the results you received

The `ip route` call on the host system outputs the system's routing table. On my current VPS, this is:
```
$ ip route
default via 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto static metric 100
<first 3 octets of the VPS' public IPv4 address>/24 dev ens3 proto kernel scope link src <VPS' IPv4 address> metric 100
172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto static scope link metric 100
```

The `ip route` call inside the container outputs a table without entries for the gateway and default route, i.e.
```
# ip route
<first 3 octets of the VPS' public IPv4 address>/24 dev ens3 proto kernel scope link src <VPS' IPv4 address> metric 100
```

As a result, attempts to reach the internet using IPv4 fail. IPv6 connectivity has no issues.
```
# ping -c 1 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
ping: sendto: Network unreachable
# ping -c 1 2404:e80::1337:af
PING 2404:e80::1337:af (2404:e80::1337:af): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 2404:e80::1337:af: seq=0 ttl=255 time=257.822 ms

--- 2404:e80::1337:af ping statistics ---
1 packets transmitted, 1 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 257.822/257.822/257.822 ms
```

NOTE: The `<first 3 octets of the VPS' public IPv4 address>` entry in the routing table is the result of an error in my static network configuration. I have left it in the above output because it demonstrates that parts of the routing table *do* get propagated to the container, just not the gateway and default route. In a correct configuration where that extraneous entry is not present in the host system's routing table, the container's routing table is just empty.
```
$ ip route
default via 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto static metric 100
172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto static scope link metric 100
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta alpine sh
# ip route
<no output>
```

### Describe the results you expected

I expected a complete routing table to be available inside the container, as is the case on my home network.
```
$ ip route
default via 192.168.0.1 dev end0 proto dhcp src 192.168.0.11 metric 100 
192.168.0.0/24 dev end0 proto kernel scope link src 192.168.0.11 metric 100 
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta alpine sh
# ip route show
default via 192.168.0.1 dev end0 
192.168.0.0/24 dev end0 scope link  src 192.168.0.11
# ping -c 1 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: seq=0 ttl=42 time=22.281 ms

--- 8.8.8.8 ping statistics ---
1 packets transmitted, 1 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 22.281/22.281/22.281 ms
```

### podman info output

```yaml
$ podman info

  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.30.0
  cgroupControllers:
  - cpu
  - memory
  - pids

  conmon:

    version: 'conmon version 2.1.7, commit: f633919178f6c8ee4fb41b848a056ec33f8d707d'
  cpuUtilization:
    idlePercent: 99.54

  cpus: 1

  distribution:

      host_id: 1000
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
  kernel: 6.3.1-arch2-1
  linkmode: dynamic
  logDriver: journald
  memFree: 1747542016
  memTotal: 2017873920
  networkBackend: netavark
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: /usr/bin/crun is owned by crun 1.8.3-2
    path: /usr/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version 1.8.3
      commit: 59f2beb7efb0d35611d5818fd0311883676f6f7e
      rundir: /run/user/1000/crun
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +YAJL
  os: linux
  remoteSocket:
    path: /run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: false
    capabilities: CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT
    rootless: true
    seccompEnabled: true
    seccompProfilePath: /etc/containers/seccomp.json
    selinuxEnabled: false
  serviceIsRemote: false
  slirp4netns:
    executable: /usr/bin/slirp4netns
    package: /usr/bin/slirp4netns is owned by slirp4netns 1.2.0-1
    version: |-
      slirp4netns version 1.2.0
      commit: 656041d45cfca7a4176f6b7eed9e4fe6c11e8383
      libslirp: 4.7.0
      SLIRP_CONFIG_VERSION_MAX: 4
      libseccomp: 2.5.4
  swapFree: 0
  swapTotal: 0
  uptime: 1h 3m 54.00s (Approximately 0.04 days)

  authorization: null

  - k8s-file

  graphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: extfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "true"
    Supports d_type: "true"
    Using metacopy: "false"
  imageCopyTmpDir: /var/tmp
  imageStore:
    number: 1
  runRoot: /run/user/1000/containers
  transientStore: false
  volumePath: /home/neoninteger/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes
version:
  APIVersion: 4.5.0
  Built: 1681856273
  BuiltTime: Wed Apr 19 07:47:53 2023
  GitCommit: 75e3c12579d391b81d871fd1cded6cf0d043550a-dirty
  GoVersion: go1.20.3
  Os: linux
  OsArch: linux/amd64
  Version: 4.5.0
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Reproduced in the following environments:
* Hetzner CPX21 running Fedora CoreOS (this server is now busy with something else and cannot be used for further testing)
* Hetzner CX11 running Fedora Server with Hetzner customisations (e.g. networking configured using `cloud-init`)
* Hetzner CX11 running Arch Linux (current deployment)

The CoreOS and Arch instances were custom OS deployments that did not use Hetzner's `cloud-init` system. In these systems, I tested both DHCP and manual IPv4 configuration, the issue occurs in both configurations.

The issue does *not* occur on my home network, with either of the following devices:
* MacBookPro11,1 (Arch Linux)
* Hardkernel ODROID-C4 (Fedora CoreOS, aarch64 variant)

### Additional information

It should be noted that the routing table being incomplete seems to be the only issue here. If I manually add the rules from the host system, IPv4 connectivity within the container seems to work.
```
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta --cap-add=NET_ADMIN alpine sh
# ping -c 1 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
ping: sendto: Network unreachable
# ip route add 172.31.1.1 dev ens3
# ip route add default via 172.31.1.1
# ping -c 1 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 8.8.8.8: seq=0 ttl=42 time=7.861 ms

--- 8.8.8.8 ping statistics ---
1 packets transmitted, 1 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 7.861/7.861/7.861 ms
```

comments.body:
@neoninteger thanks for reporting this!

I have no idea yet where the issue might be. First off, please note that, in general, `pasta` doesn't replicate the whole routing table by design -- the idea is just to copy enough of it as to provide quasi-host connectivity to the container. We plan to refine this a bit further, because it currently causes issues like [this one](https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=46), but I don't think it's in general feasible to have an exact copy.

In any case, your issue has little to do with this -- I'm just mentioning it as you say that the container misses some routing entries, but that might be expected in general.

My current suspicion is that the address inherited by the container is somehow not compatible with the route we're trying to add, maybe a variation of [this case](https://passt.top/passt/commit/?id=7656a6f8888237b9e23d63666e921528b6aaf950).

Could you please print out addresses outside and inside the container? It would immensely help my understanding if you could mangle the addresses by keeping consistency and using [reserved IPv4 addresses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reserved_IP_addresses#IPv4) from the `TEST-NET` classes (192.0.2.0/24, 198.51.100.0/24, or 203.0.113.0/24), instead of text descriptions. Thanks!

I should clarify that I originally posted the issue here on Podman's issue tracker instead of on the upstream Bugzilla instance because I'd tried running `pasta` directly (without arguments), then tried running `dhclient` inside the namespace, and didn't have any problems (the default route appears as expected). So I assumed the issue was Podman's doing.

I overlooked the fact that the `--config-net` option existed and was used by Podman, and can confirm that the issue is reproducible when using `--config-net` and no invocation of `dhclient`.
```
$ pasta --config-net -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw
# ip route
<no output>
# exit
$ pasta -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw
# dhclient
# ip route
default via 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 
172.31.1.1 dev ens3 scope link
```

The addresses of the gateway and host system, as well as the routing tables again, both inside and outside of a `pasta` namespace, are shown below.
```
$ ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: ens3: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 96:00:02:2d:59:8e brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    altname enp0s3
    inet 65.108.218.67/32 scope global dynamic noprefixroute ens3
       valid_lft 86347sec preferred_lft 86347sec
    inet6 2a01:4f9:c011:8389::1/64 scope global noprefixroute
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::9400:2ff:fe2d:598e/64 scope link noprefixroute
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
$ ip route
default via 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto dhcp src 65.108.218.67 metric 100
172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto dhcp scope link src 65.108.218.67 metric 100
$ ip -6 route
::1 dev lo proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
2a01:4f9:c011:8389::/64 dev ens3 proto kernel metric 100 pref medium
fe80::/64 dev ens3 proto kernel metric 1024 pref medium
default via fe80::1 dev ens3 proto static metric 100 pref medium

$ pasta --config-net -t none -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw
# ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: ens3: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65520 qdisc fq_codel state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/ether b2:83:92:fd:d7:bc brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 65.108.218.67/32 scope global ens3
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 2a01:4f9:c011:8389::1/64 scope global nodad
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::b083:92ff:fefd:d7bc/64 scope link
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
# ip route
# ip -6 route
2a01:4f9:c011:8389::/64 dev ens3 proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
fe80::/64 dev ens3 proto kernel metric 256 pref medium
default via fe80::1 dev ens3 metric 1024 pref medium
```
(After some sleep, I've come to the realisation that there's nothing of value on this testing server and I'm thus comfortable with its real IP address information being disclosed).

Thank you for posting the link to the GCE patch as the symptoms and cause do seem to be similar. The IP address provided by Hetzner's DHCP system does indeed have a `/32` sub-net mask, implying that the gateway cannot be on the same logical sub-net. In fact, the assigned IPv4 address is *very* different to the provided gateway address.
```
65.108.218.67 = 01000001 01101100 11011010 01000011
172.31.1.1    = 10101100 00011111 00000001 00000001
```

There is no possible sub-net mask for which these addresses match. So the algorithm in the patch you linked would run for all 32 iterations, shrinking the sub-net mask all the way to 0, eventually finding that the shifted addresses still don't match, and just leaving the original, broken `/32` mask.

Since there is now credible evidence that this is not specifically a Podman issue, should further discussion be moved to Bugzilla, or is keeping it here okay?

> Since there is now credible evidence that this is not specifically a Podman issue, should further discussion be moved to Bugzilla, or is keeping it here okay?

I would keep it here to avoid unnecessary indirection.

> ```
> 2: ens3: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP group default qlen 1000
>     link/ether 96:00:02:2d:59:8e brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
>     altname enp0s3
>     inet 65.108.218.67/32 scope global dynamic noprefixroute ens3
>        valid_lft 86347sec preferred_lft 86347sec
>     inet6 2a01:4f9:c011:8389::1/64 scope global noprefixroute
>        valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
>     inet6 fe80::9400:2ff:fe2d:598e/64 scope link noprefixroute
>        valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
> $ ip route
> default via 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto dhcp src 65.108.218.67 metric 100
> 172.31.1.1 dev ens3 proto dhcp scope link src 65.108.218.67 metric 100
> ```

Groan.

> Thank you for posting the link to the GCE patch as the symptoms and cause do seem to be similar. The IP address provided by Hetzner's DHCP system does indeed have a `/32` sub-net mask, implying that the gateway cannot be on the same logical sub-net. In fact, the assigned IPv4 address is _very_ different to the provided gateway address.
> 
> ```
> 65.108.218.67 = 01000001 01101100 11011010 01000011
> 172.31.1.1    = 10101100 00011111 00000001 00000001
> ```
> 
> There is no possible sub-net mask for which these addresses match. So the algorithm in the patch you linked would run for all 32 iterations, shrinking the sub-net mask all the way to 0, eventually finding that the shifted addresses still don't match, and just leaving the original, broken `/32` mask.

Right. I wonder why nowadays there seems to be an expectation for that kind of configuration to be in any way sane. In my opinion it spectacularly clashes with RFC 791 section 2.2:

>    The internet module prepares a datagram header and attaches the data
>    to it.  The internet module determines a _**local**_ network address for
>    this internet address, in this case it is the address of a gateway.

(emphasis mine). Whatever, we can't just go and "fix" that in all the possible environments.

Unless there are other preferences, I would add a workaround that configures, at least for those cases, or if there's a default route for our outbound interface without gateway, an equivalent route in the detached namespace. In your case, that would be the `172.31.1.1 dev ens3 scope link` just like `dhclient` sets it up. We need to modify `nl_route()` in `netlink.c` a bit to support gateway-less routes (but we'll need that anyway to cover this [case](https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=49)).

Feel free to send a patch, or I might get to it later today (presumably tomorrow for you).

I can reproduce this on a Hetzner cloud VPS. Maybe @hetznercloud (maybe @tomsiewert?) can and wants to give us some insights about https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18539#issuecomment-1545260780 to better understand the reasons for Hetzner's network setup? :+1:

> Feel free to send a patch, or I might get to it later today (presumably tomorrow for you).

I have some time on Tuesday (ACST) and am quite happy to give it a go if it hasn't already been done by then.

I just posted [a series](https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230514181415.313420-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/#related) that should fix this by optionally copying all the routes (and addresses) associated to the selected interface on `pasta --config-net` (enabled by default).

It turned out that it's actually simpler to do that, rather than trying to figure out if we can happily copy a single route or if we should copy more than one.

I haven't tried particularly hard to replicate the problematic setup, though, so testing and feedback would be very appreciated. Thanks!

> I haven't tried particularly hard to replicate the problematic setup, though, so testing and feedback would be very appreciated. Thanks!

I can provide access to a Hetzner cloud VPS for this purpose if you want to, just send me a SSH pubkey.

> I just posted [a series](https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230514181415.313420-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/#related) that should fix this by optionally copying all the routes (and addresses) associated to the selected interface on `pasta --config-net` (enabled by default).
> 
> It turned out that it's actually simpler to do that, rather than trying to figure out if we can happily copy a single route or if we should copy more than one.

Running Fedora 38 on a Hetzner cloud VPS and it looks like that even though the route for the gateway is now copied, the default route is not, still leaving us with an unusable setup.

<details>
<summary>System info</summary>

```console
$ cat /etc/fedora-release
Fedora release 38 (Thirty Eight)
$ uname -a
Linux ubuntu-2gb-fsn1-1 6.2.11-300.fc38.x86_64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Apr 13 20:27:09 UTC 2023 x86_64 GNU/Linux
$ ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP group default qlen 1000
    link/ether 96:00:02:30:43:a8 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    altname enp0s3
    altname ens3
    inet 49.13.6.54/32 scope global dynamic noprefixroute eth0
       valid_lft 83435sec preferred_lft 83435sec
    inet6 2a01:4f8:c010:820f::1/64 scope global noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::9400:2ff:fe30:43a8/64 scope link noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
$ ip route
default via 172.31.1.1 dev eth0 proto dhcp src 49.13.6.54 metric 100 
172.31.1.1 dev eth0 proto dhcp scope link src 49.13.6.54 metric 100 
```

</details>

Running Podman 4.5.0 with passt stable `0^20230509.g96f8d55-1.fc38`:

```console
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta alpine sh
/ # ping -c3 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
ping: sendto: Network unreachable
/ # ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65520 qdisc fq_codel state UNKNOWN qlen 1000
    link/ether 3a:fe:c0:62:60:f2 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 49.13.6.54/32 scope global eth0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 2a01:4f8:c010:820f::1/64 scope global flags 02 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::38fe:c0ff:fe62:60f2/64 scope link 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
/ # ip route
/ # 
```

Running Podman 4.5.0 with passt [96f8d55c4f5093fa59c168361c0428b53b6d2d06](https://passt.top/passt/commit/?id=96f8d55c4f5093fa59c168361c0428b53b6d2d06) and patches applied:

<details>
<summary>Building...</summary>

```console
$ git clone https://passt.top/passt
…
$ cd passt
$ b4 am -T -o- https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230514181415.313420-2-sbrivio@redhat.com/ | git am
Looking up https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230514181415.313420-2-sbrivio%40redhat.com
Analyzing 11 messages in the thread
Checking attestation on all messages, may take a moment...
---
  [PATCH 1/10] netlink: Fix comment about response buffer size for nl_req()
  [PATCH 2/10] pasta: Improve error handling on failure to join network namespace
  [PATCH 3/10] netlink: Add functionality to copy routes from outer namespace

```console
$ podman run -it --rm --network=pasta alpine sh
/ # ping -c3 8.8.8.8
PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8): 56 data bytes
ping: sendto: Network unreachable
/ # ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65520 qdisc fq_codel state UNKNOWN qlen 1000
    link/ether e6:e0:b9:e9:fc:77 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 49.13.6.54/32 scope global dynamic noprefixroute eth0
       valid_lft 83838sec preferred_lft 83838sec
    inet6 2a01:4f8:c010:820f::1/64 scope global noprefixroute 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::e4e0:b9ff:fee9:fc77/64 scope link 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
/ # ip route
172.31.1.1 dev eth0 scope link  src 49.13.6.54  metric 100 
/ # 
```

> > I haven't tried particularly hard to replicate the problematic setup, though, so testing and feedback would be very appreciated. Thanks!
> 
> I can provide access to a Hetzner cloud VPS for this purpose if you want to, just send me a SSH pubkey.

Thanks for the fast test and response! I think I figured out the issue, no need for access _at the moment_ (but thanks a lot for the offer).

> System info
>
> [...]
>
> ```
> $ ip route
> default via 172.31.1.1 dev eth0 proto dhcp src 49.13.6.54 metric 100 
> 172.31.1.1 dev eth0 proto dhcp scope link src 49.13.6.54 metric 100 
> ```
>
> [...]
>
> ```
> / # ip route
> 172.31.1.1 dev eth0 scope link  src 49.13.6.54  metric 100 
> / # 
> ```

If routes depend on each other, we might fail to add some depending on the order they are originally returned via netlink in the parent network namespace. That is, `default via 172.31.1.1 dev eth0` depends on the fact we have a route for `172.31.1.1`.

I reproduced something similar. Now, if I duplicate the `nl_route()` block adding the routes:

```
	if (op == NL_DUP) {
		char resp[NLBUFSIZ];

		nh = (struct nlmsghdr *)buf;
		nl_req(1, resp, nh, nlmsgs_size);
	}

	if (op == NL_DUP) {
		char resp[NLBUFSIZ];

		nh = (struct nlmsghdr *)buf;
		nl_req(1, resp, nh, nlmsgs_size);
	}
```

both routes are inserted: the first message triggers the insertion of the route to `172.31.1.1`, and fails inserting the default route, while the second message inserts the default route because the route to `172.31.1.1` is now there.

It's not an elegant workaround to say the least, even though it should be robust as long as we send n identical messages for n routes.

I'll try to come up with something more decent, even though I'm quite reluctant to implement a full sorting logic to sort routes there. By the way, if @neoninteger or you come up with something better before I get back to this, feel free to post reworked (or completely different) patches, or just let me know here. Thanks!

> It's not an elegant workaround to say the least, even though it should be robust as long as we send n identical messages for n routes.

By the way, on a second thought, after chatting about this with @dgibson... maybe it's just "superficially ugly", but after all it's correct. I start liking the idea to be honest.

> > It's not an elegant workaround to say the least, even though it should be robust as long as we send n identical messages for n routes.
> 
> By the way, on a second thought, after chatting about this with @dgibson... maybe it's just "superficially ugly", but after all it's correct. I start liking the idea to be honest.

The problem I see with that is the runtime complexity is O(n^2). Granted I guess most people just have the default route and the local subnet one but having 10 routes on the interface would already result in up to 100 netlink calls.

> > > It's not an elegant workaround to say the least, even though it should be robust as long as we send n identical messages for n routes.
> > 
> > 
> > By the way, on a second thought, after chatting about this with @dgibson... maybe it's just "superficially ugly", but after all it's correct. I start liking the idea to be honest.
> 
> The problem I see with that is the runtime complexity is O(n^2). Granted I guess most people just have the default route and the local subnet one but having 10 routes on the interface would already result in up to 100 netlink calls.

It's O(n) actually: n routes, n calls. Yes, potentially, n routes implies n^2 route attempted insertions, but I can't imagine that ever turning into an issue.

Running Podman 4.5.0 with passt [96f8d55c4f5093fa59c168361c0428b53b6d2d06](https://passt.top/passt/commit/?id=96f8d55c4f5093fa59c168361c0428b53b6d2d06) with the [patchset](https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20230514181415.313420-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/#related) and the following additional patch applied (new server, but weirdly enough I got the same IP address again):

```diff
diff --git a/netlink.c b/netlink.c
index 70218cd..81f2415 100644
--- a/netlink.c
+++ b/netlink.c
@@ -319,6 +319,13 @@ next:
                nh = (struct nlmsghdr *)buf;
                nl_req(1, resp, nh, nlmsgs_size);
        }
+
+        if (op == NL_DUP) {
+                char resp[NLBUFSIZ];
+
+                nh = (struct nlmsghdr *)buf;
+                nl_req(1, resp, nh, nlmsgs_size);
+        }
 }
 
 /**
```

Works! Thank you very much @sbrivio-rh :heart:

I've just spent some time testing the patch series on the mailing list with the additional patch provided in [the above comments](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18539#issuecomment-1547030353) on both my home network and VPS. I've tried models of all of the scenarios I envision using in the near-future, including:
* containers being able to reach the internet and other local network devices
* other devices on the local network and internet being able to reach forwarded TCP and UDP ports
* linking ports between containers on the same machine
* all of the above on both IPv4 and IPv6

As far as I can tell, all of the `pasta` functionality I intend to use works.

I don't know whether David's thoughts will end up changing anything or not. In case further revision of the patch series occurs, I'll stay subscribed to the mailing list and test any new relevant proposals as time allows.

Thank you for taking the time to work on this. `pasta` is proving to be an excellent solution for container networking and I look forward to continuing its use.

If any Podman maintainers wish to close this issue early, then please do so. If not, I'll close it myself once a relevant patch series has been applied and is being made available by distribution package repositories.
